content.explanation: Every DNS record has a TTL (Time To Live), in seconds, telling resolvers how long they're allowed to cache the answer before asking again. A short TTL means changes propagate faster but the authoritative server gets asked more often; a long TTL reduces load but means an old, cached answer can keep being served for a while after you change the record.
content.example:
dig crm.corp.local
;; ANSWER SECTION:
crm.corp.local.   3600   IN   A   172.16.5.20
                   ^^^^ TTL in seconds (1 hour) — a resolver won't re-check
                        this record again until that hour is up.

check.statement: Fill in the blank: before a planned IP address change, lowering a record's ____ ahead of time lets resolvers pick up the new address faster once you cut over, instead of serving the old cached one for hours.

solution.explanation: Lowering the TTL well before a cutover (a common practice called 'TTL priming') means resolvers stop trusting long-lived cached copies of the old record right around the time you make the change, so the new address propagates in minutes instead of hours.
